TextENGNew: At the very moment I glanced at my watch wondering what time it was, the alarm went off. My appointment with my father was almost upon me.
changeReason: Old says the appointment time had arrived; new says it was 'almost upon me,' matching the JPN もうすぐ (almost/soon). The new also adds the narrator's inner thought about what time it was (今何時だろう).

TextENGNew: I descended the stone steps of the Furude Shrine, mounted the folding bicycle I had left there, and set off toward the former Sonozaki main house.
When I arrived at the meeting point, my father had just finished his investigation and was taking a rest.
We exchanged our respective materials and cross-checked to make sure neither of us had missed anything.
Good — it looked like we were in the clear for now.
All that remained was to return to Tokyo and report our findings at the grave of Ooishi-san — a friend my father had made here in Hinamizawa.
changeReason: Old implies reporting before returning to Tokyo; new correctly follows JPN order of returning to Tokyo first, then reporting at the grave.

TextENGNew: I was sure he would be glad to hear it...
"...Hm?@k@vS10/40/430100018. That mobile phone — does it actually get a signal here?"
I had happened to notice the man my father and the others called "Captain" making a call, and once he had finished, I waited and asked.
changeReason: Old says only 'my father' referred to him as captain; new correctly includes 'my father and the others' (父たちから), indicating multiple people used this title.

TextENGNew: For a moment his expression read 'what on earth do you mean?' but he quickly understood and gave a nod. "Yes."
"There's no relay station in this area, is there?@k@vS10/40/430100020. Or does the signal reach even out here?"
"Hahaha, no, nothing like that.@k@vS10/00/VX_taicho_0002. This is a satellite phone."
Saying that, the Captain pulled the phone back out of the breast pocket he had been slipping it into and handed it to me.
changeReason: Old implies the phone was already in the pocket; new correctly captures that the Captain was mid-action of putting it away (入れかけた = was about to put it in), then reversed the motion.

TextENGNew: At first glance it looked like a perfectly ordinary mobile phone, but the design and the manufacturer's logo were ones I had never seen before.

"It uses communication satellites, so it can connect even in the middle of the ocean.@k@vS10/00/VX_taicho_0004. The cost is quite steep, though, so it's not really practical for private use unless you travel abroad a great deal."
"I see... If only I had signed up for one and brought it with me."
If I had, any number of the difficulties from last night might have been resolved in an instant.
We could have avoided dragging ordinary civilians into a dangerous situation, and I could have been spared that cold sweat...
"......@k@vS10/40/430100023. No — wait. That's not right."
It was precisely because of that time — those difficulties — that a life that need not have been lost was saved, and a future that had been sealed off by despair was opened again.
It was the sort of convenient backward logic where the end justifies the means — but sometimes, I supposed, that was simply how things worked.
changeReason: Old renders 結果オーライ as simply 'nice to think of the end result,' losing the narrator's self-aware acknowledgment of convenient hindsight logic; new captures the self-deprecating tone of 'the end justifies the means.'

TextENGNew: "...Oh, right. I'm sorry.@k@vS10/40/430100025. Would you mind if I borrowed that for a moment?"
"? Of course, but... what for?"

"Actually... I just remembered there was something my supervisor told me to report on urgently.@k@vS10/40/430100027. I'd planned to call once I got back to the hotel, but it's now been a full two days..."
"Ah, I see.@k@vS10/00/VX_taicho_0007. By all means, please go ahead."
He raised no objection on the grounds of it being official government equipment; the Captain agreed readily.
changeReason: Old incorrectly says the captain had no reason to refuse because it was for official purposes; new correctly conveys that the Captain didn't refuse even though the phone was for official (government) use, matching the JPN nuance.

TextENGNew: After excusing myself with a quick "pardon me," I stepped a short distance away and called the director.
Unfortunately, the director was in a far angrier mood than I had anticipated.
I kept bowing my head (not that she could see it) and explained the situation as best I could, but she simply said "Get the data and your report to me immediately," and hung up unilaterally.
"That's a problem..."
I let out an involuntary sigh.
I had remembered until the morning two days ago that the data I had prepared was needed for tomorrow's meeting.
I had powered through several nights without sleep to finish it, and all that remained was to send it off.
changeReason: Old says 'worked all night' (singular); new says 'powered through several nights without sleep,' correctly reflecting 徹夜続き (consecutive all-nighters).

TextENGNew: But then I had caught wind that Ryuunosuke-san — that is, Mr. Arakawa — was setting out for Hinamizawa on an investigation, and in all the bustle of preparing to go myself... it had simply slipped clean out of my head.
Fortunately I had brought my PDA along as a precautionary data archive — but for now, there was nothing to be done about it.
It was a nuisance, but it looked like I would need to return to Okinomiya for the time being...
When I told my father I was heading back ahead of him, the Captain extended the satellite phone I had just returned to him back toward me, and offered a helpful alternative.
"If you connect a terminal to it, you can use it for data transfer as well.@k@vS10/00/VX_taicho_0009. Rather than making the trip back to Okinomiya, why not just send it from here?"
"Oh... are you sure that's all right?"
Lucky.
Taking advantage of his generosity once more, I pulled my PDA and a communications cable from my pack.
I had a modem and adapter intended for ordinary mobile phones, but would they be compatible with a satellite phone...?
Holding my breath, I lined the socket up with the satellite phone's port — and was rewarded with a clean, decisive click.
I booted up the PDA, connected the cable to its data port, and opened the mail application.
Good — it looked like it would work.
"All right, then.@k@vS10/40/430100033. ...Send."
"Oh, right.@k@vS10/40/430100035. I should send a copy to my secondary address as well.@k@vS10/40/430100035_1. And while I'm at it, to Director Minai too..."
She had told me "there's no rush until you're back," but this was one thing I wanted to get to her as quickly as possible — even one day sooner mattered.
After all, this was the most compelling "evidence" yet that the theory Director Minai had been maintaining for years was not wrong...
Good — all done.
As a precaution I called the director to confirm the files had arrived safely, then closed my PDA.
Then, practically skipping, I made my way back while trying to think of a suitable way to thank the Captain for lifting that weight from my shoulders.
Just as evening was drawing in, I returned to the Furude Shrine once more.
I passed through the remnants of the garden in the shrine grounds and made my way into the pine grove, where weeds had overgrown everything in thickets.
From the open vantage of the hilltop, the full panorama of the village lay dyed in the evening light — a scarlet so vivid it was almost startling.
changeReason: Old describes the sunset hue as 'dusky vermillion,' but 鮮やか means vivid/brilliant; new correctly captures the 'vivid crimson' quality of the scene.

TextENGNew: Taking care to check my footing so as not to slip, I stepped up to the very edge of the hilltop.
changeReason: Old says checking 'the rail to make sure it would hold'; new correctly translates 足場を確かめ as checking one's footing to avoid slipping.

TextENGNew: The wind rushing up from the base of the cliff was surprisingly strong, carrying just enough coolness to be welcome against my perspiring skin.
...This was the place that held so many of my father's lingering feelings — a place bound up with fate.
In the end, my father... had not come here after all.
At first I had thought he was simply leaving it for last as a matter of efficiency — but when I invited him to come here again, that was when I finally understood.
He could not come.

Because it was on this hilltop that a little girl had come to my father and begged for his help — and the promise he had been unable to keep had left a wound in his heart that could never truly heal——.
"Furude... Rika..."
I let the girl's name quietly cross my lips.
Long ago, when I was small, my father had told me about meeting a girl in Hinamizawa who was like an angel — and I had been captivated by the story in the way only a child can be.
changeReason: Old ambiguously uses 'her childlike curiosity' which could be misread as referring to the angelic girl; new correctly attributes the curiosity to the narrator (子供心に興味を抱いた = as a child, I became interested).

TextENGNew: And I had told him I wanted to be friends with her.
With no idea, at the time, how much grief those innocent words must have stirred in him...
I turned my gaze back down to the settlement spread below.

Looking more carefully, what had once been fields and paddies was now a solid carpet of encroaching green with no trace of the paths between them; the reservoir had grown so choked with algae it was indistinguishable from anything else.
changeReason: Old vaguely mentions 'a section greener on one side'; new accurately describes the former fields and paddies completely overgrown with no paths visible, and the reservoir choked with algae, faithfully capturing the JPN details.

TextENGNew: The thatched farmhouses still held their original shapes — but no one lived in them any longer.
A village of the dead. A ghost village.
More than twenty years ago, a tragedy like a living hell had unfolded here.
The truth of it... was still unknown.
Even the existence of the volcanic gas was doubted, and the rumors and speculation circulating everywhere could hardly be called anywhere close to the truth.
"...And even if we were to investigate, to search and search, and finally uncover the truth...@k@vS10/40/430100042."
The lives that were lost, the time that had passed — none of it would ever come back.
The dreams and hopes of over two thousand villagers.
The peaceful, unhurried days that had truly existed here more than twenty years ago.
All of it was now only a ghost of the past.

It had become something fit only for academic investigation by scholars of the past — and there was nothing in it that could heal the wounds of those who had been hurt, nothing to ease the regret and remorse they still carried.
But even so.
My father had devoted everything that remained of his life to uncovering the truth of this incident.

Even though he was about to step down from public security work and the relatively comfortable life of a police academy principal awaited him — my father was still choosing the thorny path, still pressing on in his atonement for the past.

"...Hey, Rika-san.@k@vS10/40/430100045. When will my father finally be freed from this?@k@vS10/40/430100046. What would it take for him to leave these sad feelings behind and smile — truly smile, from the bottom of his heart...?"
changeReason: Old uses 'redeem himself' (implying guilt) for 救われる which means 'be saved/freed from suffering' — different nuance about the father's situation.

TextENGNew: I let the words drift softly into the wind, carrying them toward the person who was perhaps, by now, somewhere above the sky.
No voice answered. Of course there was none.
In the end, this too was nothing more than my own sentimental self-indulgence...
Why did this tragedy come to pass?@r@vS10/40/430100048. We alone cannot@reach the truth.
You who are reading this —@r@vS10/40/430100050. please, uncover the truth.@r@vS10/40/430100051. That is our only wish.
And yet today, as always, I continue to send out messages that reach no one.
